name: Ifanês (Mirandese septentrional)
notes: Ifanês is the local speech of Ifanes (Infainç) in the northern part of Terra de Miranda; it belongs to the Northern / Raiano (border) subdialect of Mirandese. Phonologically it tracks Central Mirandese: the published descriptions record NO segmental orthography→phoneme divergence for the Northern/Raiano group. In particular, Central and Raiano BOTH keep the Leonese diphthongs ⟨iê/ie⟩ = [je] and ⟨uô/uo⟩ = [wo] (only Sendinês monophthongises them to [i]/[u]), and BOTH keep the palatal lateral /ʎ/ where Sendinês has /l/. This is corroborated by the small Raiano gold sample (raiano 'fuogo' → [ˈfwo.ʊ], diphthong retained). The Raiano/Northern distinctives that ARE reported are morphosyntactic and lexical (definite-article forms, some nasalisation and syntactic constructions) — out of scope for a grapheme→IPA spec. This spec therefore inherits the full Central Mirandese system unchanged and is grounded by documentation rather than by a distinct rule set; an earlier draft's monophthongisation ⟨iê⟩→[e] was unsourced and is removed as contradicted by both the literature and the Raiano gold.

sources.author: Vasconcelos, J. Leite de
sources.title: Estudos de Philologia Mirandesa (Vol. I)
sources.notes: Foundational description that first delimits the Mirandese subdialects (central, the northern/raiano speech of the Miranda plateau, and Sendinês) and their phonology.

sources.author: Ferreira, M.B. & Raposo, D. (coords.)
sources.title: Convenção Ortográfica da Língua Mirandesa
sources.publisher: Câmara Municipal de Miranda do Douro / Centro de Linguística da Universidade de Lisboa
sources.notes: Official orthography, built on the Central/Raiano norm; records that the ⟨iê/uô⟩ spelling reflected the [je]/[wo] pronunciation of the Central and Raiano dialects (reduced to [i]/[u] only in Sendinês).

sources.author: Mateus, M.H.M. & d'Andrade, E.
sources.title: The Phonology of Portuguese
sources.notes: Reference for the shared Ibero-Romance processes (nasal place assimilation, spirantisation) inherited from the Central spec.

stress.notes: Convenção Ortográfica da Língua Mirandesa (1999) accentuation, Portuguese-based: paroxytone default; written accent (acute or tilde) overrides. Oxytone-attracting endings reflect Mirandese orthography: consonant-final r/l/z/ç (rapaç, Pertual, falar) and the final nasals written with -n in the Asturleonese manner — -in (camin, Sendin), -un, and the systematically oxytone -on class from Latin -ōnem (naçon, coraçon, Associaçon). Unmarked -an/-en verb endings stay paroxytone as in Portuguese -am/-em. Dialect mwl-x-ifanes: no evidence of stress-pattern divergence from standard Mirandese; block copied from mwl.
